title: State-building: the Greek polis
description: Answer questions based on a passage relating to Greek-city states.

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the following question, re-read this section of the article:

> "Though the Greek city-states were unified to some extent in the face of an external threat, as that threat waned, conflicts between the city-states made a resurgence. Following the [Persian] wars, Athens emerged as the supreme naval power in Greece. It formed the Delian League, ostensibly to create a cohesive Greek network among city-states to ward off further Persian attacks. Under the leadership of Pericles, Athens grew so powerful that the Athenian Empire could effectively dictate the laws, customs, and trade of all her neighbors in Attica and the islands of the Aegean. 

> The might of the Athenian Empire encouraged an arrogance in the policy makers of the day which grew intolerable to the other city-states. When Athens sent troops to help Sparta put down a Helot rebellion, the Spartans refused the gesture and sent the Athenian force back home in dishonor, thus provoking the war which had long been brewing. Later, when Athens sent their fleet to help defend its ally Corcyra (Corfu) against a Corinthian invasion during the Battle of Sybota in 433 BCE, their action was interpreted by Sparta as aggression instead of assistance, as Corinth was an ally of Sparta."

**Why might being a strong naval power have aided Athens in controlling many of its neighbors?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Athens' neighbors weren't more concerned about fending off threats from Persia.

Why might being a strong naval power have aided Athens in controlling many of its neighbors?

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Athens’ neighbors were more concerned with fending off threats from Persia, giving Athens the opportunity to exploit them.
Many of the other nearby city-states were located on small islands or coastal peninsulas; a strong navy could quickly project power into those places and cut off support.
Many of the other nearby city-states likely saw Athens as a powerful force and respected its naval control.
Athens had the ability to disrupt trade in the area, causing economic disadvantages for its neighbors.

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the following question, re-read this section of the article:

> "Though the Greek city-states were unified to some extent in the face of an external threat, as that threat waned, conflicts between the city-states made a resurgence. Following the [Persian] wars, Athens emerged as the supreme naval power in Greece. It formed the Delian League, ostensibly to create a cohesive Greek network among city-states to ward off further Persian attacks. Under the leadership of Pericles, Athens grew so powerful that the Athenian Empire could effectively dictate the laws, customs, and trade of all her neighbors in Attica and the islands of the Aegean. 

> The might of the Athenian Empire encouraged an arrogance in the policy makers of the day which grew intolerable to the other city-states. When Athens sent troops to help Sparta put down a Helot rebellion, the Spartans refused the gesture and sent the Athenian force back home in dishonor, thus provoking the war which had long been brewing. Later, when Athens sent their fleet to help defend its ally Corcyra (Corfu) against a Corinthian invasion during the Battle of Sybota in 433 BCE, their action was interpreted by Sparta as aggression instead of assistance, as Corinth was an ally of Sparta."

** What could explain Sparta's rebuke of Athens’ offer of troops during the Helot uprising?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Sparta probably didn't think they had too many troops; it's likely that more troops would mean a higher chance at victory.

choices.content: Spartan leaders felt that the revolt was theirs to put down, and that the offer of help was a condescending insult.
Spartans felt their troops were better trained and they had no need for inferior Athenian troops.
Spartan leaders felt they already had too many troops and additional soldiers would disrupt their formations.
Since Athens was mainly a naval power, Spartan leaders were offended they sent ground troops instead of ships and naval soldiers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the following question, re-read this section of the article:


> "Though the Greek city-states were unified to some extent in the face of an external threat, as that threat waned, conflicts between the city-states made a resurgence. Following the [Persian] wars, Athens emerged as the supreme naval power in Greece. It formed the Delian League, ostensibly to create a cohesive Greek network among city-states to ward off further Persian attacks. Under the leadership of Pericles, Athens grew so powerful that the Athenian Empire could effectively dictate the laws, customs, and trade of all her neighbors in Attica and the islands of the Aegean. 

> The might of the Athenian Empire encouraged an arrogance in the policy makers of the day which grew intolerable to the other city-states. When Athens sent troops to help Sparta put down a Helot rebellion, the Spartans refused the gesture and sent the Athenian force back home in dishonor, thus provoking the war which had long been brewing. Later, when Athens sent their fleet to help defend its ally Corcyra (Corfu) against a Corinthian invasion during the Battle of Sybota in 433 BCE, their action was interpreted by Sparta as aggression instead of assistance, as Corinth was an ally of Sparta."


**Based on the information provided, which of the following statements would the author most likely agree with?**


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: The article excerpt says that the city-states unified to some extent in order to tackle external threats, such as Persia.

choices.content: Being part of the Delian League allowed the other poleis of the Aegean to keep Athens's power in check.
Sparta established a powerful hegemony in response to the Persian Wars, controlling much of Attica.
Even war with Persia could not unite the city-states of Greece, even temporarily.
Athens was a unified empire but problems with diplomacy caused friction with Sparta.

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the next question, study this map:

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1ca4112ef459618c15afb410ea94c88c.png)

**What do you think the author's purpose is in including this map?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Although the map shows where Homeric characters came from, that wasn't the purpose of showing the map in the article.

choices.content: She's arguing that Ancient Greece was composed of many city-states which, despite their geographic proximity, maintained distinct political identities
She’s providing literary context by showing the readers where various Homeric characters came from
She’s showing why a strong Athenian naval power wouldn’t have been particularly helpful to controlling the region
She’s showing how the fractured nature of the region made it possible for Persia to conquer Greece
